title: Quantum Statistics and Structural Topology Govern Thermal Transport in Two-Dimensional Monolayer Amorphous Carbon
abstract: We investigate the quantum thermal conductivity (TC) of two-dimensional monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC). We employ three distinct amorphization algorithms to generate various possible MAC configurations, ranging from Zachariasen-type continuous random networks to nanocrystallites embedded in random networks. The local bond order parameter, q3, is used to quantify the amorphousness of the structures, and TC is computed as functions of q3 and temperature. This framework enables us to assess how structural topology, degree of amorphization, and quantum statistics contribute to heat conduction in a two-dimensional amorphous solid. At room temperature, TC values are predicted to range between 3.5 to 10 W/m/K, in agreement with recent experiments. Analysis of vibrational modes reveals that, while the modes of these 2D amorphous structures fall into the usual categories, namely, propagons, diffusons, and locons, their polarization characteristics display distinct behavior. Owing to the fully quantum mechanical framework, we examine both low- and high-temperature characteristics of this 2D amorphous system. By examining the classical limit, we show that classical treatments substantially overestimate the TC of MAC; namely, the quantum TC is less than half of the classical value at room temperature and up to nearly an order of magnitude lower at low temperatures.
summary: This work theoretically investigates heat conduction in 2D amorphous carbon by coupling structural disorder ($q_3$) with quantum statistics. Using the Landauer formalism, the authors show that quantum effects significantly suppress thermal conductivity compared to classical models. The findings establish that the overall degree of amorphization is the dominant factor controlling heat flow in these disordered quantum materials.
main_problem: The study investigates how structural topology, degree of amorphization, and quantum statistics govern the thermal transport in two-dimensional amorphous solids.
main_result: Quantum thermal conductivity is significantly lower than classical predictions, and the thermal transport is primarily governed by the global degree of amorphization ($q_3$) rather than specific microscopic structural details.
method: The authors employ a fully quantum mechanical framework using the Landauer formalism and Green's function techniques to calculate thermal conductivity ($\kappa$).
model_or_system: The physical system is two-dimensional monolayer amorphous carbon (MAC). Configurations are generated using various amorphization algorithms (e.g., Zachariasen-type networks) and characterized by the local bond order parameter, $q_3$.
key_observables: Quantum Thermal Conductivity ($	ext{TC}$), Participation Ratio ($	ext{PR}$), Local bond order parameter ($q_3$), and vibrational mode characteristics (propagons, diffusons, locons).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), degree of amorphization ($q_3$), and the ratio of classical to quantum conductivity ($\kappa_{cl}/\kappa_q$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Landauer formalism and assumes the system can be modeled by partitioning it into a central amorphous region and semi-infinite crystalline reservoirs.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate representative MAC structures, the dependence of $\kappa$ on $T$ and $q_3$, and comparisons between quantum and classical conductivity predictions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes structural characterization (using $q_3$ and bond statistics), vibrational mode analysis (using PR and polarization spheres), and finally calculates $\kappa(T)$ by comparing quantum (Bose-Einstein) and classical limits.
why_interesting: The rigorous quantum mechanical treatment of heat transport in amorphous solids, particularly the comparison between quantum and classical limits, provides insights into energy dissipation mechanisms in disordered quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Continuous random network (CRN) and embedded nanocrystallite (NC@RN) structures obtained using 3C-GM, 3C, and nC algorithms are shown in the left, center, and right panels. The colors represent the value of the local bond order parameter, q3. The average q3 value is 0.6 and it is the same for all structures shown in (a). Radial distribution function (b), bond length distribution (c), bond angle distribution (d), and ring statistics (e) of the configurations with different structural types and two different q3 parameters are shown in the lower panels.
Figure 2: Mode participation ratios (PRs) for 3C-GM and NC@RN structures with order parameter values of q3 = 0.60 and q3 = 0.75.

Figure 3: (a) Polarization spheres of 2D crystalline graphene (all modes) and amorphous carbon (certain frequency ranges) contrast the directionality of crystalline and amorphous structures in 2D. (b) Atomic displacements and corresponding polarizations of four selected modes illustrate the distinctive features of propagons, diffusons and locons. 20

Figure 4: Classical and quantum mechanical weight functions are plotted as a function of x = ℏω/kBT (a). The classical weight function is equal to unity independent of temper- ature, whereas the quantum mechanical one peaks at x = 0 and approaches pcl at the high-temperature limit. Transmission (b) and conductivity (c) for different structure types and q3 values. Blue, red, and green colors represent 3C-GM, 3C, and nC schemes, while dotted, solid, and dashed curves stand for q3 values of 0.7, 0.55, and 0.4, respectively. In (b), the horizontal lines depict thermal conductivity values at the classical limit.
Figure 5: The ratio of classical and quantum thermal conductivities in MAC with q3 = 0.55 is plotted as a function of temperature (main plot), and at room temperature for varying q3 values (inset).
